title: Supraventricular Tachycardia in Infant
chief_complaint: My baby is breathing fast, won't feed, and is very pale and sweaty. She's been like this for 6 hours. She's getting worse.

physical_exam.General: Pale, diaphoretic, lethargic infant. Poor perfusion.
physical_exam.Cardiac: Extreme tachycardia (280 bpm) - too fast to count. Rate is REGULAR (key for SVT vs sinus tachycardia). No murmurs.

hidden_data.ecg: Narrow complex tachycardia 280 bpm. P waves NOT visible (buried in T waves - typical for AVRT/AVNRT). Rate REGULAR. No beat-to-beat variability (differentiates from sinus tachycardia).
hidden_data.echo: Structurally normal heart. No CHD. Mildly reduced LV function from prolonged tachycardia (tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy).
hidden_data.adenosine_response: Adenosine 0.2mg/kg rapid IV push: Tachycardia terminates abruptly → sinus rhythm 140 bpm with visible P waves. Confirms AV node-dependent SVT (AVRT or AVNRT).

correct_diagnosis: Supraventricular Tachycardia (AVRT/AVNRT) in Infant with Acute Decompensated Heart Failure
key_learning_points: SVT = most common symptomatic arrhythmia in children. Infant SVT: HR >220, regular, narrow QRS, no P waves visible (or retrograde P waves)
Differentiate from sinus tachycardia: SVT >220 in infants, REGULAR, abrupt onset/offset, no HR variability. Sinus tachycardia <220, varies with stimulation, P waves present upright in I and aVF

Infants with SVT can decompensate quickly: Poor feeding, tachypnea, pallor, lethargy. Prolonged SVT (>24h) → tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy and CHF. This patient has early CHF (hepatomegaly, gallop, mild LV dysfunction)

Acute treatment: HEMODYNAMICALLY STABLE: Vagal maneuvers (ice to face - diving reflex in infants - most effective). Adenosine 0.1-0.3mg/kg rapid IV push (first-line). Synchronized cardioversion 0.5-1 J/kg if adenosine fails. UNSTABLE (shock, severe CHF): Synchronized cardioversion 0.5-1 J/kg immediately

Chronic management: Propranolol or Atenolol for infants (safe, effective). Flecainide or Sotalol for refractory. Digoxin for CHF (but avoid in WPW - can precipitate VF). Catheter ablation reserved for older children if medical therapy fails. Most infants outgrow SVT by 12 months
